title: grantableModelItems misclassifies ModelRouterResponse aliases as 'local' in the access picker
issue: The structural narrowing `else if ('alias' in alias)` matches not only UserAliasResponse and ModelAliasResponse (real local GGUF aliases) but also ModelRouterResponse (a composite routing alias), because ModelRouterResponse also carries an `alias` string field. The backend confirms ModelRouter variants are included in the PaginatedAliasResponse returned by GET /bodhi/v1/models. The wrongly-classified ModelRouterResponse items are assigned `type: 'local'` and land in the 'Local Models' group in the picker panel with a 'local' badge.
failure_scenario: A user creates a ModelRouter alias named 'my-router'. On the New Token or review-consent screen, the model picker panel shows 'my-router' under the 'Local Models' group with a 'local' badge instead of in a neutral (untyped) group. If the user applies a 'Local only' type filter in the picker, 'my-router' incorrectly appears in the results. If they apply 'API only', it is correctly excluded — but it is a valid grant target that they would miss. The grant ID itself is correct (the alias string), so enforcement still works, but the UX is misleading.
recommendation: Add a third branch before the `else if ('alias' in alias)` branch to detect ModelRouterResponse structurally (e.g., `'targets' in alias && 'strategy' in alias`) and emit an item with no `type` field (or a new `'router'` type), so it renders in the untyped group rather than 'Local Models'. No type needed in AccessItem for now — just omit `type` to keep it neutral.
rationale: The models list endpoint includes all four AliasResponse variants. The current check only discriminates ApiAliasResponse correctly; the remaining three variants (User, Model, ModelRouter) all match the `'alias' in alias` guard. ModelRouter aliases are inference-capable endpoints and valid grant targets, so they must appear in the picker — but their visual classification is wrong.

title: AccessPickerPanel group labels are model-specific strings ('Local Models', 'API Models') hardcoded regardless of noun
issue: When items carry a `type` field, the slide-in panel always groups them under the labels 'Local Models' and 'API Models', ignoring the `noun` prop. This makes the shared component model-specific. If future grant categories (e.g., local vs hosted tools) needed type-based grouping, the labels would be wrong.
failure_scenario: N/A — architecture/convention issue. Currently MCP items have no `type`, so `hasTypes` is false for the MCP panel and the model-specific labels are never shown for MCPs. Risk is triggered only when typed non-model items are added.
recommendation: Derive group labels from noun: `Local ${noun[0].toUpperCase() + noun.slice(1)}s` / `API ${noun[0].toUpperCase() + noun.slice(1)}s`, or accept optional `localGroupLabel`/`apiGroupLabel` props defaulting to the current strings.
rationale: The component is explicitly designed for sharing (comment: 'Shared by models and MCPs') but contains model-specific strings, creating a maintenance trap for future resource types.

title: Apps hooks use inline error extraction instead of the shared extractErrorMessage utility, silently dropping network error details
location: useRevokeAppAccess line 97, useDenyAppAccessRequest line 121, useApproveAppAccessRequest line 64
issue: The three new/modified mutations in the apps hooks extract error messages inline (`error?.response?.data?.error?.message || 'Failed to ...'`) instead of calling `extractErrorMessage(error, 'Failed to ...')` as the tokens hooks do. The inline version only reads the Bodhi API envelope. On a network failure (CORS block, timeout, server down) where `error.response` is undefined, the inline version swallows the underlying AxiosError message and always shows the hardcoded fallback. `extractErrorMessage` also reads `error.message` for non-envelope errors before falling back.
failure_scenario: The user is on the App Tokens screen and the backend is unreachable (network down). They click 'Revoke Access' and confirm. The network call fails with an AxiosError whose `.message` is 'Network Error'. The hook fires `onError('Failed to revoke app access')` instead of 'Network Error', hiding the real cause from the toast.
recommendation: Import and call `extractErrorMessage(error, 'Failed to ...')` in place of the three inline expressions, matching the pattern in useTokens.ts.
rationale: useTokens.ts already uses extractErrorMessage consistently. Diverging from the shared utility in newly added hooks increases the surface area where error messages are degraded.

title: ReviewContent fetches models and MCPs unconditionally, even when the app requested neither grant type
issue: `useListModels` and `useListMcps` are always called at component mount regardless of whether the access request includes `models_access`, `models_list`, `mcps_access`, or `mcps_list` flags. React-Query rules prevent conditional hook calls, but the enabled option could be set to `false` when the flags are absent. Currently two extra API calls fire for every consent review even if the GrantBlock sections are never rendered.
failure_scenario: N/A — correctness/architecture issue. The excess calls succeed silently and the data is just unused. The GrantBlock sections only render when the flags are set, so the populated `modelItems`/`mcpItems` are discarded.
recommendation: Set `enabled` based on reviewData's flags: `useListModels(1, 100, 'alias', 'asc', undefined, { enabled: !!(reviewData && (req?.models_access || req?.models_list)) })`. This requires waiting for `reviewData` before enabling, which React-Query supports natively.
rationale: Aligns data fetching with actual usage — only load the picker datasets when the review screen will actually show the pickers.

title: No unit tests for grantableModelItems / grantableMcpItems in grantItems.ts
issue: The shared bucketing logic in grantItems.ts — which maps AliasResponse variants to AccessItem grant IDs — has no test file. It is the single source of truth for what models/MCPs appear in the picker and under which type group. Edge cases like ModelRouterResponse classification, duplicate ID deduplication (the Map), an ApiAliasResponse with a null prefix, and an empty models array are untested.
failure_scenario: N/A — test-coverage issue. The ModelRouterResponse misclassification bug (finding #1) exists in production with no failing test to catch it.
recommendation: Add `src/lib/grantItems.test.ts` covering: all four AliasResponse variant kinds, ModelRouterResponse mapped to a no-type or neutral-type item, duplicate IDs deduplicated, ApiAliasResponse with null prefix, empty MCP list, and MCP item ID=name mapping.
rationale: grantItems.ts is called from both TokenForm and the review consent screen, making it a high-leverage shared function. A regression here silently breaks grants for all users on both surfaces.

title: E2E spec missing positive enforcement paths for granted model/MCP access
location: line 31 — the single test covers only the no-model grant → 403 negative path
issue: Phase 9 in the plan explicitly required: 'granted model infers, other model 403; granted MCP connects, other 403; /v1/models & /v1/mcps reflect toggles'. The implemented spec approves a grant of *zero* models and *zero* MCPs, then only asserts the 403 on inference. There is no test step that grants a specific model, confirms that model infers (200), and confirms a different model is rejected (403). There is no test step that grants a specific MCP and confirms it connects. There is no assertion against /v1/models or /v1/mcps to verify the listing-filter reflection.
failure_scenario: If AccessPolicy.allows_model_inference regressed to always-true for ExternalApp, the existing spec would still pass because it only tests the all-denied path. Similarly, if mcp_listable silently allowed all MCPs after the bespoke mcps_index filter was deleted, no E2E step would catch it.
recommendation: Add a second scenario (or extend the existing test with steps): approve a grant of one specific model + one specific MCP with list_models=true, list_mcps=true. Then assert: GET /v1/models returns only the granted model; GET /v1/mcps returns only the granted MCP; POST /v1/chat/completions with the granted model returns 200 (not 403); POST /v1/chat/completions with a *different* model returns 403; MCP proxy to the granted instance returns success; MCP proxy to a *different* instance returns 403.
rationale: The plan phase 9 gate explicitly listed positive-path enforcement as a required coverage item. Without it, regressions in AccessPolicy that break the All-allow/deny split for ExternalApp would not be detected by E2E.

title: page.evaluate used for clipboard assertion in TokensPage.copyTokenFromDialog
location: lines 238-239 — copyTokenFromDialog reads window.clipboardData via page.evaluate
issue: The plan's Phase 9 and the E2E CLAUDE.md both mandate 'UI-only interactions (no page.evaluate / context fetch)'. copyTokenFromDialog uses `await this.page.evaluate(() => window.clipboardData)` to read the clipboard content for an assertion. This is a black-box violation: test assertions must be driven by observable DOM state (testids / data-test-state), not by JavaScript evaluation inside the page. Additionally, the method places an unconditional `page.waitForTimeout(100)` before the evaluate — an inline timeout, which is also banned by the E2E conventions.
failure_scenario: In a headless Chromium run that uses a real navigator.clipboard (no mock injected), window.clipboardData would always be undefined and copyTokenFromDialog silently returns undefined. Any test step that relied on the returned value (e.g., line 62 copiedToken = await tokensPage.copyTokenFromDialog()) would get undefined, making subsequent chat-token assertions pass vacuously rather than failing loudly.
recommendation: Replace the page.evaluate read with a DOM-observable assertion: the token reveal dialog already exposes the token via data-testid='token-value-input-content'. Show the token (toggleShowToken), read its textContent, then assert clipboard via an expected DOM indicator (e.g., data-testid='copied-content' appears after copy — as the unit test already asserts). Remove the waitForTimeout(100) and use expect(page.locator('[data-testid="copied-content"]')).toBeVisible() as the wait gate.
rationale: The established convention (E2E.md 'Black-box only' rule and plan phase 9) forbids page.evaluate for assertions. The existing unit test (TokenDialog.test.tsx:53-65) already verifies clipboard copy through navigator.clipboard.readText() — the E2E should rely on the DOM indicator the component renders after copying.

title: Inline waitForTimeout used in TokensPage.toggleTokenStatus
issue: E2E.md explicitly bans `page.waitForTimeout()`: 'No inline timeouts: No page.waitForTimeout() or setTimeout(). Wait for UI state changes via data-testid/data-test-state attributes'. toggleTokenStatus clicks the status switch then calls waitForTimeout(100) before returning. On a slow CI machine the 100ms is insufficient; on a fast machine it introduces unnecessary latency. The correct pattern is to wait for an observable UI state change.
failure_scenario: On a loaded CI agent where the token list refetch takes >100ms to update the switch's aria-checked attribute, any caller that checks status immediately after toggleTokenStatus (e.g., expectTokenStatus in the api-tokens.spec.mjs multi-user isolation test) reads stale state and the assertion intermittently fails.
recommendation: Remove waitForTimeout(100). After clicking the switch, wait for the toast that confirms the status change (already tested in the spec: waitForTokenUpdateSuccess). The token-status switch already has aria-checked reflecting the new state once the mutation completes; Playwright's built-in retry in subsequent assertions handles the transition.
rationale: The E2E convention explicitly forbids inline timeouts and instructs waiting for UI state changes instead. waitForTimeout is the primary source of flaky E2E tests noted in the codebase.

title: Consent approval payload test does not assert mcps_list field
location: lines 1056-1069 — body assertion in 'approve payload carries the model + MCP grants'
issue: The test captures the raw PUT body from the approve handler and asserts models_list (line 1064), models_access (line 1066), mcps[0].status (line 1067), and mcps_access (line 1069). It does NOT assert body.approved.mcps_list. The fixture mockDraftWithGrantFlagsResponse includes mcps_list: true in the requested flags and the UI renders the 'review-list-mcps-toggle' (verified at line 1004). The user can toggle it on/off, but the resulting value in the approved payload is never checked. If the component omits mcps_list from the payload or always sends false, the test passes.
failure_scenario: If toggleListMcps() in the consent review component had a bug that set mcps_list=false in the approved body even after the toggle is checked on, the test at line 1046 (clicking review-list-models-toggle) would succeed, the MCP select + approve flow would succeed, and the assertion block would pass — because mcps_list is not in the assertion type cast or expect calls.
recommendation: Extend the assertion block to include `expect(body.approved.mcps_list).toBe(true)` (since the test clicks review-list-models-toggle and the fixture has mcps_list: true). Add a companion test where mcps_list is toggled OFF and verify the payload carries mcps_list: false. Mirror the existing models_list assertion pattern.
rationale: Both list toggles are symmetric UI controls. Asserting models_list but not mcps_list creates an asymmetric coverage hole; a regression on the MCP listing toggle would slip through the unit test layer entirely.

title: ListingToggle keyboard activation (Space/Enter) not unit-tested
location: line 25 — only click toggling is tested; no keyboard test exists
issue: ListingToggle.tsx:41-47 implements an onKeyDown handler that fires onToggle for Space and Enter keys. The test file has 4 tests covering render, click-toggle, redundant-hint, and disabled-click-no-fire. No test covers keyboard activation. The component is rendered as a div with role=checkbox and tabIndex=0, so keyboard interaction is the primary accessible path.
failure_scenario: N/A — convention: the onKeyDown handler at ListingToggle.tsx:43 fires onToggle on Space/Enter when not disabled; if this code path regressed (e.g., key check changed from 'Space' to ' ' or vice versa) no unit test would detect it.
recommendation: Add two unit tests: one that fires userEvent.keyboard(' ') on the toggle element and asserts onToggle is called; one that fires userEvent.keyboard('{Enter}') and asserts onToggle is called. Also add a disabled variant that asserts neither key fires onToggle (mirrors the existing 'does not toggle when disabled' click test).
rationale: The component explicitly advertises keyboard accessibility (role=checkbox, tabIndex=0). Testing only click paths leaves the keyboard handler as dead code from the test's perspective.

title: AccessPickerPanel type filter (Local/API dropdown) not unit-tested
location: line 74 — search filter is tested; type filter select is not
issue: AccessPickerPanel.tsx:38-48 implements a typeFilter state and a <select> (data-testid='{prefix}-panel-type') that appears when any item has a type property. The ITEMS fixture in the test already has mixed types (local: llama3:8b, mistral:7b; api: gpt-4o). The panel-type select is rendered but never exercised in the tests. The filtering logic (lines 44-45) is covered by the search test only incidentally.
failure_scenario: N/A — convention: if typeFilter filtering logic regressed (e.g., swapped local/api labels), no unit test would catch it. The model token form (where items have both local and api types from grantableModelItems) renders the dropdown in production but the unit harness never selects from it.
recommendation: Add a test that opens the panel, selects 'Local' from the type filter select, and verifies only local items appear (llama3:8b, mistral:7b visible; gpt-4o hidden). Add a companion test for 'API' filter.
rationale: The type filter is the primary UX differentiator between the model picker (which mixes local + API models) and the MCP picker (no types). It is new code in this diff and has no coverage.

title: GrantBlock component has no dedicated unit test
issue: GrantBlock.tsx (new in this diff) is a composition wrapper around ListingToggle + AccessPicker that supports showListing=false and showAccess=false to selectively render each half — the consent screen uses these to show only the controls the app requested. There is no GrantBlock.test.tsx. The showListing=false / showAccess=false props are exercised indirectly through the consent screen unit test (review/index.test.tsx), but the component's own rendering contract is not directly tested.
failure_scenario: N/A — convention: if showListing=false accidentally still rendered the ListingToggle (e.g., condition inverted), the consent screen test might still pass if it only checks for the presence of the wrapper testid and not the absence of the toggle.
recommendation: Add GrantBlock.test.tsx with at minimum: renders both halves by default; showListing=false hides the listing toggle but shows the picker; showAccess=false shows the toggle but hides the picker; disabled=true passes disabled to both child components. Keep it shallow (no MSW needed).
rationale: GrantBlock is the shared seam between two critical screens (token form and consent review). Testing its conditional rendering contract directly makes the component resilient to future props changes.

title: TokenForm scope_token_power_user card disabled state not tested for resource_user role
location: line 99 — TokenForm describe block; no test for disabled Power User card
issue: TokenForm.tsx:83 computes canPowerUser from userInfo.role and sets the Power User role card disabled when the user is resource_user (line 202). The test's beforeEach mounts mockUserLoggedIn({}, { stub: true }) which returns the default role. There is no test verifying that when userInfo.role is 'resource_user', the scope_token_power_user card has disabled=true and cannot be clicked to change scope.
failure_scenario: N/A — convention: if the canPowerUser guard regressed (e.g., condition always true), a resource_user could create a scope_token_power_user token via the UI. No unit test would detect this elevation.
recommendation: Add a test using mockUserLoggedIn({ role: 'resource_user' }) that asserts scope-card-scope_token_power_user has attribute disabled and clicking it does not change the selected scope. Add a complementary test for resource_power_user where both cards are enabled.
rationale: The canPowerUser guard is a client-side enforcement of a role boundary. While the server enforces the same rule, unit-testing the client guard ensures regressions are caught at the fastest feedback level.

title: HTTP verb mismatch: approve uses PUT while deny and revoke use POST
location: utoipa annotation line 227 (put) and crates/routes_app/src/routes.rs line 354 (put(apps_approve_access_request))
issue: The three owner-facing state-transition actions on an access request use inconsistent HTTP verbs: approve is PUT (both in utoipa and the actual Axum route binding), while deny and revoke are POST. All three are one-shot non-idempotent transitions (a second call returns 409 Conflict), so none of them satisfy the RFC definition of idempotency that would justify PUT. Client SDKs and API consumers must hard-code the different verb for approve vs the other two actions.
failure_scenario: A generated SDK client driven by the OpenAPI spec calls `PUT /bodhi/v1/access-requests/{id}/approve` (correct) but a consumer who infers the verb from the sibling `POST .../deny` endpoint and mistakenly issues `POST /bodhi/v1/access-requests/{id}/approve` gets a 405 Method Not Allowed instead of the expected 404/409/200. A client testing the deny-then-approve flow will hit the wrong verb and get an unexpected 405.
recommendation: Change `approve` to POST to match deny and revoke: in `routes_apps.rs` update the utoipa annotation from `put,` to `post,`; in `routes.rs` change `put(apps_approve_access_request)` to `post(apps_approve_access_request)`. All three state-transition action endpoints on the same resource family should use the same verb.
rationale: REST convention: POST is correct for non-idempotent action endpoints that change state (approve/deny/revoke are all one-shot). PUT implies idempotency. Consumers and auto-generated SDKs derive the verb from the OpenAPI spec, so the mismatch creates a correctness hazard for any client that handles these actions uniformly.

title: GET /bodhi/v1/user reflects grants at different JSON depths for ApiToken vs ExternalApp
location: routes_users_info.rs lines 118-132 (ApiToken arm) and lines 155-172 (ExternalApp access block)
issue: Effective grant information is surfaced at structurally different paths in the JSON response depending on the principal type. For auth_status='api_token': models and mcps are at the root level (flattened from TokenInfo). For auth_status='logged_in' with ExternalApp context: models and mcps are one level deeper inside the optional 'access' sidecar field. There is no field in the response that explicitly identifies the auth_status='logged_in' case as an ExternalApp rather than a browser session (only the presence of 'access' hints at this). A consumer checking root.models gets the correct answer for API tokens but misses the ExternalApp grant entirely.
failure_scenario: A proxy service or UI component calls GET /bodhi/v1/user and reads `response.models` to determine what models the caller can access. For an API-token caller this works correctly. For an ExternalApp caller the field is absent at root level (models is undefined); the actual grant is at `response.access.models`. The service incorrectly concludes the ExternalApp has unrestricted access and skips the model filter, or crashes dereferencing undefined.
recommendation: Document the asymmetry explicitly in the utoipa description for UserInfoEnvelope: state that 'access' is present only for ExternalApp principals and that ApiToken principals embed their grants directly in the root object. Consider whether a future API version should unify the grant location (e.g. always emit 'access' for any restricted principal so consumers check one path). For now, TypeScript client consumers should use a discriminant function that checks both locations.
rationale: API consumers building grant-aware code must branch on auth_status to find the correct JSON path for model/MCP access. The OpenAPI schema for UserInfoEnvelope marks 'access' as optional without documenting when it appears vs. is absent, which forces client authors to read source code rather than the spec.

title: Collection endpoint GET /access-requests/apps placed inside the per-ID action namespace
issue: All other paths under /bodhi/v1/access-requests/ are per-resource ID actions: /access-requests/{id}/review, /access-requests/{id}/approve, /access-requests/{id}/deny, /access-requests/{id}/revoke. The new collection endpoint for listing issued app grants uses the literal path segment 'apps' in the same namespace, mixing a collection route with per-resource routes. This makes 'apps' look visually like an {id} value rather than a distinct sub-resource or collection, and means GET /access-requests/apps/revoke would route to the revoke handler with id='apps' (returning 404 from the DB, not 405). The parallel API token collection lives at /bodhi/v1/tokens (a top-level resource), not nested under another resource's action paths.
failure_scenario: N/A — design/convention: a developer exploring the spec expects /access-requests/{id} to be the only dynamic segment and is surprised to find a static 'apps' sibling that is semantically a collection endpoint, not an ID action.
recommendation: Move the collection to a cleaner namespace: /bodhi/v1/apps/tokens (parallel to /bodhi/v1/tokens for API tokens) or /bodhi/v1/apps/grants. This separates the owner-facing management collection from the per-request lifecycle actions and aligns with the existing /bodhi/v1/apps/* external-app namespace.
rationale: The /access-requests/ namespace is established for lifecycle actions on specific requests. A collection endpoint in the same namespace creates a naming collision risk and violates the established pattern. The parallel API-token management route /bodhi/v1/tokens is a top-level collection — App token management should be similarly placed.

title: OperationId 'approveAppsAccessRequest' uses plural 'Apps' breaking the naming pattern of sibling operations
issue: The four owner-facing action/query endpoints on access requests use inconsistent OperationId naming: createAccessRequest, getAccessRequestStatus, getAccessRequestReview — no 'Apps' qualifier. approveAppsAccessRequest — adds plural 'Apps'. denyAccessRequest — no qualifier. listAppAccess, revokeAppAccess — singular 'App', different suffix style. Auto-generated TypeScript client method names will be approveAppsAccessRequest (method name contains 'Apps') vs denyAccessRequest (no 'Apps'), creating an inconsistent SDK surface.
failure_scenario: N/A — convention: auto-generated TS SDK exposes approveAppsAccessRequest() vs denyAccessRequest() which is confusing for client authors writing access-request lifecycle code.
recommendation: Rename to approveAccessRequest (drop 'Apps') to match the denyAccessRequest pattern; or rename all four to include 'Apps' consistently: createAppAccessRequest, getAppAccessRequestStatus, getAppAccessRequestReview, approveAppAccessRequest, denyAppAccessRequest. Also align listAppAccess and revokeAppAccess to the chosen convention.
rationale: SDK auto-generation derives method names from operationId. Inconsistent casing/qualifiers produce an SDK where the caller must remember 'approve has Apps in the method name but deny does not', which is a friction point that compounds across every generated client language.

title: Anthropic handler `ensure_model_inference` call not covered by a handler-level test
issue: `anthropic_messages_create_handler` calls `ensure_model_inference` at line 148, but `test_anthropic_messages.rs` has no test for the 403 path (scoped API token or ExternalApp accessing a non-granted model). The OAI chat handler added analogous tests (`test_chat_completions_scoped_token_forbidden` and `test_chat_completions_external_app_model_forbidden`), but the Anthropic handler has nothing equivalent. Removing the call at line 148 would break no test.
failure_scenario: An API token with `ModelGrant::Specific { ids: ["claude-3-opus"] }` calls `POST /anthropic/v1/messages` with `model: "claude-3-sonnet"`. Expected: 403. Without a test, the regression is silent if the guard is accidentally dropped.
recommendation: Add two handler-level tests in `test_anthropic_messages.rs` mirroring the OAI pattern: one for `AuthContext::ApiToken` with a mismatched `ModelGrant::Specific` and one for `AuthContext::ExternalApp` with a mismatched `ApprovedResourcesV1::models_access`, both asserting `StatusCode::FORBIDDEN`.
rationale: The OAI handler and `test_token_grants.rs` prove the `AccessPolicy` logic is correct, but they do not exercise the Anthropic handler wiring. A test here serves as a regression net specifically for the handler.

title: Gemini handler `ensure_model_inference` call not covered by a handler-level test
issue: `gemini_action_handler` calls `ensure_model_inference` at line 213, but `test_gemini_routes.rs` has no test for the 403 path under a scoped API token or ExternalApp. All 23 existing Gemini tests use an unrestricted session context. If line 213 were deleted, no test would break.
failure_scenario: An API token with `ModelGrant::Specific { ids: ["gemini-1.5-pro"] }` calls `POST /v1beta/models/gemini-1.5-flash:generateContent`. Expected: 403. Without a test, a regression here is undetected.
recommendation: Add a handler test in `test_gemini_routes.rs` with a scoped `ApiToken` targeting a model not in its grant, asserting `StatusCode::FORBIDDEN` from `generate_content_action_handler`.
rationale: Same pattern confirmed needed for OAI and Anthropic; the Gemini handler is missing the same protection.

title: MCP proxy `ensure_mcp_connect` enforcement has no handler-level test
issue: `mcp_proxy_handler` enforces `ensure_mcp_connect` at line 70, but there is no test file for the proxy handler at all. The enforcement is only validated as a pure unit test in `test_token_grants.rs`. Removing line 70 from the handler would break no test.
failure_scenario: An API token with `McpGrant::Specific { ids: ["mcp-allowed"] }` calls `POST /bodhi/v1/apps/mcps/mcp-forbidden/mcp`. Expected: 403. No test exists at any layer that would catch a regression in this handler path.
recommendation: Create `test_mcp_proxy.rs` (or a test section within an existing file) with a test that wires `mcp_proxy_handler` under a scoped token with a mismatched MCP grant, asserting `StatusCode::FORBIDDEN` without making an upstream call (mock `McpService.get()` to return a valid entity).
rationale: The proxy is the only path for actual MCP traffic; grant enforcement here is the runtime security boundary. Untested handler wiring is a regression risk.

title: `mcps_show` grant-hidden 404 branch not tested
issue: `mcps_show` returns a synthetic 404 for any MCP id not in the caller's grant (to avoid leaking existence). The router in the test includes `mcps_show`, but none of the tests exercise this early-return branch. The filtering test only covers the list endpoint.
failure_scenario: A scoped `ApiToken` with `McpGrant::Specific { ids: [] }` calls `GET /bodhi/v1/apps/mcps/{id}` for an existing MCP. Expected: 404. Actual: 200 with the MCP details (existence leak) if the guard were removed — no test would catch it.
recommendation: Add a test in `test_mcps.rs` that routes a `GET /mcps/{id}` request through a scoped token lacking the grant for that MCP id, asserts `StatusCode::NOT_FOUND`, and verifies `MockMcpService.get()` was never called.
rationale: This is the `mcps_show` equivalent of `test_oai_model_handler_scoped_token_hides_ungranted`. The list tests do not imply the detail endpoint also gates correctly.

title: `update_revocation` repository method has no unit tests
issue: `update_revocation` is a new method added in this diff with business-critical guard logic embedded in the repository layer: (a) tenant filter on the find query, (b) owner-only check (`row.user_id != caller_user_id → ItemNotFound`), (c) Approved-only gate (`status != Approved → AccessRequestNotDraft`). None of these branches are covered by any test in `test_access_request_repository.rs`. The route-handler test covers the happy path and the owner-mismatch case through the full stack, but the non-Approved rejection and tenant-level filter are not exercised end-to-end.
failure_scenario: Developer calls `update_revocation(tenant_b, id_owned_by_tenant_a, user)`. The SQLite path (no RLS) relies on the explicit `Column::TenantId.eq(&tenant_id_owned)` filter; if that filter were dropped, the row would be found and revoked across tenants. No repository-level test would catch this.
recommendation: Add to `test_access_request_repository.rs`: (1) happy-path Approved→Revoked, (2) wrong user → ItemNotFound, (3) non-Approved status (e.g., Draft) → AccessRequestNotDraft error, (4) cross-tenant get returns ItemNotFound. Use `#[values("sqlite", "postgres")]` following the existing isolation test pattern.
rationale: Business-critical guard logic (tenant isolation, owner-only, state-machine guard) lives in the repository impl and must be directly tested at that layer.

title: `list_approved_for_user` repository method has no unit tests
issue: `list_approved_for_user` is a new method added in this diff. It filters by `status == Approved` and `user_id`. No test in `test_access_request_repository.rs` verifies: (a) rows with status Revoked/Denied/Draft are excluded, (b) rows belonging to a different user in the same tenant are excluded, (c) ordering is newest-first. The route-handler test confirms the empty-list-after-revoke behavior but does not seed multiple status variants to test the filter itself.
failure_scenario: A Revoked request that should be excluded is returned because the `Status.eq(Approved)` filter was accidentally omitted. The handler test only checks the list returns 0 items after a revoke of the only item; it doesn't distinguish 'excluded by status filter' from 'not in DB'.
recommendation: Add a test that inserts one Approved, one Revoked, and one Draft request for the same user, calls `list_approved_for_user`, and asserts only the Approved row is returned. Add a second case with two users in the same tenant to verify user scoping.
rationale: The status and user filters are correctness requirements; without a test that distinguishes them from the broader state, silent filter regressions are invisible.

title: `retain_listable_models` has no unit tests in the services layer
issue: `retain_listable_models` is a new method with four distinct branches: `User`, `Model`, `ModelRouter` (single-model keep), and `Api` (which splits further on `forward_all_with_prefix`). The `Alias::Api` branch with `forward_all_with_prefix = false` does in-place pruning of the inner `models` list and returns `false` (drop the alias) when the list becomes empty. This is the most complex path and is tested neither as an inline unit test in `model_objs.rs` nor in `test_models.rs` with a scoped token + API alias fixture. The route handler test `test_oai_models_scoped_token_filters_listing` uses only `Alias::User`/`Alias::Model` aliases from the data service fixture.
failure_scenario: A scoped token (`ModelGrant::Specific { ids: ["openai/gpt-4o"] }`) lists models when an `ApiAlias` has `prefix="openai/"` and `models=[gpt-4o, gpt-3.5-turbo]`. `retain_listable_models` should keep `gpt-4o` and drop `gpt-3.5-turbo`, returning `true`. If the retain call were accidentally written as `a.models.retain(|m| !keep(...))` (negated), both models could be dropped and the alias removed. No test would catch this.
recommendation: Add inline unit tests for `retain_listable_models` in `model_objs.rs` covering: (1) `Api` with `forward_all_with_prefix=false`, some models kept → returns `true` with pruned list; (2) all models filtered out → returns `false`; (3) `forward_all_with_prefix=true` judged by prefix; (4) `User`/`Model`/`ModelRouter` pass-through. A route-handler test with a real `ApiAlias` + scoped token would also strengthen coverage.
rationale: This is the only non-trivial branch that mutates state (in-place `models.retain`). Correctness cannot be inferred from the other branches.

title: Cache eviction side-effect in revoke handler not verified
issue: `apps_revoke_access_request` calls `cache_service().remove_entries_containing(&needle)` after the DB update. `test_revoke_access_request_transitions_to_revoked` uses the real `MokaCacheService` (the default in `AppServiceStubBuilder`) but never pre-seeds the cache with a matching exchange result or asserts the cache entry is gone after revocation. If the `remove_entries_containing` call were deleted from the handler, the test would still pass.
failure_scenario: N/A — this is a test-coverage gap, not a runtime correctness issue. The unit test in `cache_service.rs` confirms the eviction algorithm is correct; what's missing is the integration between the revoke handler and the cache service.
recommendation: In the revoke handler test, pre-seed the `MokaCacheService` with a key whose value contains the `access_request_cache_needle("ar-revoke")` substring, call the revoke endpoint, and assert the cache key is subsequently absent. This requires accessing the `CacheService` from the `AppService` after the request completes.
rationale: The eviction is the primary motivation for the handler calling the cache service — it makes revocation take effect before the 5-minute TTL. An integration test confirms the wiring, not just the algorithm.

title: models_access / mcps_access field-name type collision: bool in request, ModelGrant/McpGrant in approved
issue: The field name `models_access` is typed `bool` in `RequestedResourcesV1` (a UI-driver flag meaning 'render the model picker?') but `ModelGrant` in `ApprovedResourcesV1` (the actual approved grant). Same collision for `mcps_access`: `bool` vs `McpGrant`. This is reflected verbatim in `openapi.json` (confirmed: `RequestedResourcesV1.models_access` is `type: boolean`; `ApprovedResourcesV1.models_access` is `$ref: ModelGrant`) and in `ts-client/src/types/types.gen.ts` (lines 1495 and 346 respectively). TypeScript prevents confusion at compile time, but the OpenAPI schema exposes both structs at the same level and any non-TS client (Python, Rust, curl) that reads `requested.models_access` and naively copies the field to `approved.models_access` will send `true` (bool) where a `{type:"all"}`/`{type:"specific",ids:[...]}` object is required.
failure_scenario: A Python or curl client building the consent-approve PUT body reads `GET /apps/access-requests/:id/review` → `requested.models_access = true` (bool). It then constructs the approve payload as `{"approved": {"models_access": true, ...}}`. The Rust backend attempts to deserialize `true` as a `ModelGrant` tagged enum and returns a 400/422 deserialization error, silently failing the approval.
recommendation: Rename one side to break the collision. The cleanest fix is to rename `RequestedResourcesV1.models_access` → `request_models_access` (or better: `show_models_access`) and `mcps_access` → `show_mcps_access`, making the 'this is a UI flag' intent explicit and removing the name overlap. Alternatively prefix all request flags with `want_` (e.g., `want_models_list`, `want_models_access`). Update `openapi.json`, `ts-client`, frontend, and E2E fixtures. The rename is a breaking change to the app-request API surface; version the `RequestedResources` envelope (V2) if external clients already use the old names.
rationale: Same field name with different JSON types in sibling schemas that appear together in the same API flow is a classic client-trap. The Rust type system and TypeScript prevent it internally, but any generated client or hand-rolled code in another language will see only the field name. The fix should land before external apps adopt the consent API.

title: `mcps` field means McpGrant in TokenGrantsV1 but Vec<McpApproval> in ApprovedResourcesV1
issue: Both `TokenGrantsV1` and `ApprovedResourcesV1` expose a JSON field named `mcps`, but with completely unrelated types: `McpGrant` (`{type:'all'}`/`{type:'specific',ids:[...]}`) in the API-token envelope vs `Vec<McpApproval>` (array of `{url, status, instance?}` consent records) in the app-approval envelope. In `ts-client/src/types/types.gen.ts` this manifests as `TokenGrantsV1.mcps?: McpGrant` (line 1731) vs `ApprovedResourcesV1.mcps?: Array<McpApproval>` (line 349). Developers and code-gen tools that discover both schemas by field name will conflate them.
failure_scenario: A developer writes a generic function `getMcpGrant(envelope)` that accesses `envelope.mcps`, intending to check whether a grant covers a given MCP id. For a `TokenGrantsV1` this yields a `McpGrant` with an `allows(id)` method. When they accidentally pass an `ApprovedResourcesV1`, `mcps` is an array of approval records — `mcps.allows is not a function`. TypeScript catches this only if both types are in scope; a JS or OpenAPI-generated client will silently produce wrong values.
recommendation: Rename `ApprovedResourcesV1.mcps` to `mcps_by_url` (or `mcp_approvals`) to distinguish the url-consent list from the grant-style `mcps_access: McpGrant`. This makes the two fields semantically self-documenting and eliminates the collision. Update the Rust struct, `#[serde]` rename attribute, `openapi.json`, `ts-client`, consent review handler, and all callers of `v1.mcps` in `routes_app` (approve validation, `app_mcps()`, test fixtures). It is a breaking change to the approve request body shape.
rationale: The collision surfaced because `mcps` is used as the natural shorthand for two different things in the two envelopes. With the domain-first renaming (commit 8844c135) the gap widened: `ApprovedResourcesV1.mcps_access` now clearly names the 'extra' grant, but the legacy `mcps` array kept its bare name while `TokenGrantsV1.mcps` is the main grant field. Renaming closes a documentation and code-review trap.

title: TokenGrantsV1 uses verb-first field names (list_models/list_mcps) while app envelopes use domain-first (models_list/mcps_list)
issue: After the `refactor(app-tokens): align request/response field names (domain-first)` commit (8844c135), the app envelopes use domain-first naming (`models_list`, `mcps_list`, `models_access`, `mcps_access`) while `TokenGrantsV1` — which implements the same `ResourceGrants` trait — retained verb-first naming (`list_models`, `list_mcps`, `models`, `mcps`). This inconsistency appears in `openapi.json` (`TokenGrantsV1.list_models` at line 12662 vs `ApprovedResourcesV1.models_list` at line 8814) and in `ts-client/src/types/types.gen.ts` (lines 1728-1731 vs 345-353). Developers working across both API surfaces must remember two different naming conventions for the same concept.
failure_scenario: N/A — convention inconsistency; no runtime defect. A developer reading OpenAPI docs for both endpoints incorrectly looks for `models_list` in a `TokenGrants` response and finds it undefined (the correct field is `list_models`), causing a silent no-op in listing-toggle logic.
recommendation: Standardise `TokenGrantsV1` to domain-first: rename `list_models` → `models_list`, `list_mcps` → `mcps_list`, `models` → `models_access`, `mcps` → `mcps_access`. This requires a `#[serde(rename = ...)]` or a serde `rename_all` attribute update, migration of the stored JSON column (`api_tokens.grants`), updating all Rust callers, regenerating OpenAPI and ts-client, and migrating frontend fixtures. Given the stored JSON format concern, a versioned deserializer that accepts both names during a transition window is safest. However, if `TokenGrantsV1` is considered intentionally independent of the app envelopes (as the doc comment says), the inconsistency can be accepted as a documented design choice.
rationale: The comment on `TokenGrantsV1` says 'Intentionally standalone — NOT shared with the App-access-request envelope; the two may diverge.' If that divergence is intentional and permanent, this inconsistency is by design. It is reported as nice-to-have because the two envelopes now implement the same `ResourceGrants` trait and appear together in the OpenAPI schema, making the naming difference a discoverability hazard even if it is not a correctness bug.

title: AccessRequestNotDraft reused as revoke status guard — misleading error code surfaces to clients
location: Line 408, inside update_revoke: return Err(DbError::AccessRequestNotDraft { id, status })
issue: DbError::AccessRequestNotDraft was created for the approve/deny path (Draft->Approved/Denied transition). In the newly-added update_revoke method the same variant guards the Approved->Revoked transition. The generated error code is db_error-access_request_not_draft (HTTP 409). Semantically the guard means 'the request must be in Approved state to be revoked' but the code name tells clients 'the request is not in Draft state', which is the wrong semantic.
failure_scenario: A caller invokes POST /bodhi/v1/access-requests/{id}/revoke on a request that is already Revoked, Denied, or Expired. The server returns HTTP 409 with code db_error-access_request_not_draft. A client that matches on the error code to display a message or branch logic receives the wrong signal: the code implies 'this request was never submitted (not in Draft)' rather than 'this grant is not in a revocable state'.
recommendation: Add a distinct DbError variant for the revoke guard, for example: AccessRequestNotRevocable { id: String, status: String } with error_meta Conflict. The code becomes db_error-access_request_not_revocable which correctly conveys 'cannot revoke in current state'. The three existing uses of AccessRequestNotDraft at lines 178, 242, 306 (approve/deny paths requiring Draft status) remain correct.
rationale: Error codes are the machine-readable contract for client error handling. The existing variant name AccessRequestNotDraft encodes the wrong precondition for the revoke semantic, and was reused rather than a purpose-specific variant introduced.

title: AccessPolicy::of pierces version wrapper via .v1() despite ResourceGrants being the intended abstraction seam
location: Lines 29 and 33, AccessPolicy::of: grants.v1() for ApiToken and ApprovedResources
issue: The module doc states that handlers never match on AuthContext or grant internals: all allow/deny decisions flow through AccessPolicy via a &dyn ResourceGrants. However, AccessPolicy::of itself calls .v1() on both TokenGrants and ApprovedResources to obtain the inner V1 struct, bypassing the versioning abstraction. Neither TokenGrants nor ApprovedResources implements ResourceGrants, so the factory must know which version to unwrap. Five call sites in total use .v1() across routes_app; the two in of() are the ones that could be eliminated if the versioned envelopes implemented the trait.
failure_scenario: N/A — architecture gap. When a V2 variant is added to TokenGrants or ApprovedResources, AccessPolicy::of must be found and updated manually (two separate match arms). The versioning abstraction that the module claims to provide is incomplete: the factory is version-aware, not version-agnostic.
recommendation: Add impl ResourceGrants for TokenGrants and impl ResourceGrants for ApprovedResources that delegate to the inner type via match self { Self::V1(v1) => v1.method(...) }. Then AccessPolicy::of becomes: ApiToken -> AccessPolicy::Grants(grants), ExternalApp(Some(grants)) -> AccessPolicy::Grants(grants), without any version-specific .v1() call. The other three .v1() call sites in routes_users_info.rs and apps_api_schemas.rs are justified because they need concrete struct fields beyond the trait interface, so they remain.
rationale: The ResourceGrants trait is the stated abstraction boundary for resource policy questions. That the two versioned envelope types implementing it at only V1 level forces callers to reach inside the version wrapper; implementing the trait on the envelope types fulfills the stated design and makes future version addition safe at the factory level.

title: TokenGrantsV1 field names diverge from the domain-first convention applied to ApprovedResourcesV1 in this diff
location: Lines 36-42, TokenGrantsV1 struct fields: list_models, models, list_mcps, mcps
issue: Commit 8844c135 (part of this diff) renamed the App approval fields to domain-first order: models_list, models_access, mcps_list, mcps_access in both RequestedResourcesV1 and ApprovedResourcesV1. TokenGrantsV1 was left with the old pre-rename names (list_models, models, list_mcps, mcps). The code comment acknowledges the two may diverge, but the divergence now exists on a naming axis (ordering convention) rather than a structural one, which creates schema asymmetry in the OpenAPI output.
failure_scenario: N/A — API schema asymmetry. A developer or API consumer comparing the TokenDetail.grants schema against the ApproveAccessRequest.approved schema in the OpenAPI spec sees inconsistent field naming for equivalent concepts: list_models vs models_list, models vs models_access.
recommendation: If the rename is intended as a project-wide convention, align TokenGrantsV1 to models_list / models_access / mcps_list / mcps_access and provide a DB migration that updates stored JSON in the api_tokens.grants column (a one-time script or a seamless in-place deserialize-with-alias transition). If the divergence is intentional and permanent, document it explicitly in the TokenGrantsV1 struct comment so future contributors do not attempt to harmonize the two schemas.
rationale: The inconsistency was introduced as a side-effect of applying the domain-first rename only to the App approval side. Since both schemas appear in the same OpenAPI spec and represent semantically parallel constructs, the naming divergence is an API consistency concern for SDK and documentation consumers.

title: Missing cross-tenant isolation tests for update_revocation and list_approved_for_user
issue: Two new DB-mutation methods added in this diff — `update_revocation` and `list_approved_for_user` — have no cross-tenant isolation tests. The existing isolation file (`test_access_request_repository_isolation.rs`, 72 lines) was not modified at all in this diff. Every other tenant-scoped repository operation in this codebase (token delete, MCP CRUD, etc.) has a companion isolation test following the pattern `test_cross_tenant_*` with `#[values("sqlite", "postgres")]`. The implementation does include explicit `TenantId.eq(tenant_id_owned)` filters in both methods, so the isolation logic is correct — but the required test layer is absent.
failure_scenario: A future refactor or copy-paste error removes the `TenantId.eq()` filter from `update_revocation` or `list_approved_for_user`. User A in tenant A calls `POST /bodhi/v1/access-requests/<id>/revoke` where `<id>` belongs to user A in tenant B. Without the isolation test, the cross-tenant revocation (or cross-tenant list leak) goes undetected. Because the routes_app handler test (`test_revoke_access_request_not_owner_rejected`) only tests same-tenant cross-user isolation, a cross-tenant regression would slip through all unit tests and only surface at E2E.
recommendation: Add two isolation tests to `test_access_request_repository_isolation.rs` following the existing pattern: (1) `test_cross_tenant_revoke_blocked` — seed an approved request in tenant B, call `update_revocation(TENANT_A, id, user_id)`, assert `ItemNotFound`, confirm the record is still Approved in tenant B. (2) `test_cross_tenant_list_approved_isolation` — seed approved requests in both tenants for the same user_id, call `list_approved_for_user(TENANT_A, user_id)`, assert only tenant A records appear. Both should be `#[values("sqlite", "postgres")]`.
rationale: This codebase's documented convention (crates/CLAUDE.md §Multi-Tenant Isolation Test Pattern) requires isolation tests for all new tenant-scoped DB operations. The two new methods are invoked by security-critical routes (revoke + list management), so a tenant-filter regression would be a privilege-escalation defect.

title: AppAccessSummary::from_row silently drops ApprovedResources parse errors with no diagnostic log
issue: `from_row` uses `.and_then(|json| serde_json::from_str::<ApprovedResources>(json).ok())`, which swallows any deserialization error silently. When the `approved` column contains syntactically or semantically invalid JSON (e.g., after a failed migration or schema evolution), the summary is returned with `models: Specific{list:false, ids:[]}` and `mcps: Specific{list:false, ids:[]}` while `status: Approved`. No warning is logged. By contrast, the token-exchange path (`handle_external_client_token`) correctly fails closed with `TokenError::InvalidToken` on the same parse failure. The inconsistency means an operator seeing the management UI cannot distinguish between 'intentionally granted no access' and 'DB record is corrupt'.
failure_scenario: A migration bug or schema evolution writes an invalid `approved` JSON blob to a row (e.g., `{"version":"1","models_access":"bad"}`). The token-exchange path rejects the app token with a 401, but the owner's App Tokens management screen shows the grant as `Approved` with no model or MCP access — indistinguishable from a legitimately empty-grant record. No log entry alerts on-call. The operator has to manually inspect the DB to diagnose.
recommendation: Add a `tracing::warn!` inside the `and_then` closure when deserialization fails, logging the row `id` and the raw JSON length, before calling `.ok()`. E.g.: `and_then(|json| serde_json::from_str::<ApprovedResources>(json).map_err(|e| { tracing::warn!(row_id = %row.id, err = %e, "failed to parse approved grants for display"); e }).ok())`. This keeps the fail-safe display behavior (empty access) while making the data corruption visible in logs.
rationale: The token-exchange path is the security enforcement point and correctly fails closed. The display path is read-only and showing empty-access is safe. However, silent failure makes production debugging very difficult; a one-line warn suffices to surface the issue without changing behavior.

title: Cache eviction needle relies on undocumented serialization format contract between two non-adjacent modules
issue: The `access_request_cache_needle` function in `token_service.rs` produces `"access_request_id":"<id>"` — a raw substring of the compact serde_json serialization of `CachedExchangeResult`. The eviction call in `routes_apps.rs` (a different module) invokes `remove_entries_containing(&needle)` using this value. The correctness of the eviction depends on three undocumented assumptions: (1) `CachedExchangeResult` is always serialized with `serde_json::to_string` (compact, no spaces); (2) the JSON field name is exactly `access_request_id` (no rename/alias); (3) the field value is a plain string (no escaping needed for ULID characters). The code comment acknowledges (1) but does not test any of these properties. If any future struct change (field rename, serializer swap, new string field whose value contains the needle) breaks the match, revocations silently stop being immediate — there is no compile-time or test-time signal.
failure_scenario: A developer renames the `access_request_id` field in `CachedExchangeResult` to `ar_id` for brevity, updates the struct and its usages, but misses the needle function. All subsequent revocations appear to succeed (HTTP 200, DB status = Revoked) but the cache is never evicted. The external app's previously-cached token continues to work for up to 5 minutes after revocation. The failure is invisible — no error, no test failure — until someone notices the TTL gap.
recommendation: Add a compile-time or test-time binding between the needle and the struct field. Options in ascending effort: (A) add a unit test that serializes a `CachedExchangeResult` with a known `access_request_id` and asserts `.contains(access_request_cache_needle(id))` on the result; (B) derive the needle dynamically from the struct via a short helper that serializes a sentinel value and extracts the relevant fragment, then compares against the format-string result. Option A is a one-file addition to `test_token_service.rs` and is sufficient to catch the rename scenario.
rationale: Revocation immediacy is a key security property of this design (without it, revoked grants silently remain active for up to 5 minutes). The eviction mechanism is the only path to immediate effect; if it silently stops working, users have no indication. A regression test that ties the needle to the actual serialized form would make this contract explicit and catch regressions at CI time.

title: update_revocation uses AccessRequestNotDraft error code when the check is "not approved"
issue: When update_revocation finds the record is not in Approved state (e.g., already Revoked or still Draft), it returns DbError::AccessRequestNotDraft { id, status }. The error code emitted is db_error-access_request_not_approved is what it *should* say, but the actual code is db_error-access_request_not_draft. The condition guarded is status != Approved, not status != Draft. The two checks elsewhere (update_approval, update_denial) that reuse this variant are correct because they really do guard 'not draft'; here the variant name is semantically inverted.
failure_scenario: User calls POST /access-requests/:id/revoke on a grant that was already revoked. The response body carries error code db_error-access_request_not_draft and message 'Access request X has status revoked and cannot be updated.' A client that switches on error codes to display a human-readable reason or to retry will interpret this as a draft-state problem (the request was never approved?) rather than a duplicate-revoke problem.
recommendation: Add a new DbError variant AccessRequestNotApproved { id: String, status: String } (mirroring AccessRequestNotDraft) with an appropriate error message such as 'Access request {id} has status {status} and can only be revoked when Approved.' Use it at lines 408-411 in update_revocation.
rationale: Using the wrong error variant is an observable API contract inconsistency. Callers that inspect the error code for programmatic decisions will misclassify the failure mode. The HTTP 409 status is correct but the code field is wrong.

title: TokenDetail::from silently falls back to all-access when grants JSON is corrupt, inconsistent with auth middleware fail-closed path
issue: serde_json::from_str(&t.grants).unwrap_or_default() falls back to TokenGrants::default() (all-access: list_models=true, models=All, list_mcps=true, mcps=All) when the stored grants column cannot be parsed. The auth middleware (token_service.rs) introduced in this diff does the opposite: it fails closed with TokenError::InvalidToken on a corrupt grants column. The two code paths are now inconsistent: the management UI (GET/LIST /tokens) displays all-access while every auth attempt using that token gets rejected.
failure_scenario: The grants column of a row in api_tokens is accidentally corrupted (e.g., partial write during DB migration, manual admin edit). GET /tokens/{id} and GET /tokens list responses show grants:{list_models:true, models:{type:'all'}, ...} — all-access. The user sees no indication of a problem. Every API call using that token returns 401/InvalidToken because the middleware fails closed. The user is stuck debugging a token that looks fine in the UI but never works.
recommendation: Either (a) propagate the parse error in TokenDetail::from by returning Result<TokenDetail, TokenServiceError> and letting the LIST/GET handlers surface a 500 for truly corrupt data, or (b) change the fallback to TokenGrants::V1(TokenGrantsV1 { list_models: false, models: ModelGrant::Specific { ids: vec![] }, list_mcps: false, mcps: McpGrant::Specific { ids: vec![] } }) (empty-deny) so the display is conservative and hints at a problem rather than implying all-access. Option (b) keeps the infallible From impl and matches the fail-closed philosophy of the auth path.
rationale: Display and authentication paths now give opposite answers for the same corrupt row. While the auth path correctly refuses access, the display path can mislead users into thinking the token is fully functional, delaying diagnosis of a corruption issue.

title: update_revocation and list_approved_for_user have no repository-level unit tests
location: entire file — update_revocation and list_approved_for_user are absent
issue: Both new DefaultDbService methods introduced in this diff — update_revocation and list_approved_for_user — have zero coverage in test_access_request_repository.rs. The repository test file covers create, get, update_approval, update_denial, update_failure, and get_by_access_request_scope, but not the two new methods. The only indirect coverage is through routes_app integration tests (test_revoke_access_request_transitions_to_revoked, test_revoke_access_request_not_owner_rejected, test_revoke_non_approved_request_rejected) which run at the HTTP layer against a mock-seeded DB. Critical logic paths tested nowhere: (1) double-revoke of an already-Revoked record; (2) list_approved_for_user only returns status=Approved rows and excludes Revoked; (3) tenant isolation in update_revocation (the explicit TenantId filter that defends SQLite); (4) the wrong-user check inside update_revocation (row.user_id.as_deref() != Some(user_id)) returning ItemNotFound rather than Forbidden.
failure_scenario: N/A — test-coverage gap. A regression that accidentally removed the tenant filter from the update_revocation query would allow any authenticated user in any tenant to revoke any other user's grant. This specific regression would be invisible until an E2E or manual test caught it, because no unit-level repository test exercises cross-tenant or cross-user revocation isolation.
recommendation: Add rstest-based repository tests for: (1) list_approved_for_user returns only Approved rows (not Draft/Revoked); (2) update_revocation happy path transitions to Revoked; (3) update_revocation for wrong user returns ItemNotFound; (4) update_revocation on already-Revoked returns AccessRequestNotDraft (and after the fix recommended above, AccessRequestNotApproved); (5) update_revocation for a request in a different tenant returns ItemNotFound (tenant isolation). Use the same #[values("sqlite", "postgres")] pattern as existing tests.
rationale: The revocation path is security-critical: it controls whether an app token stays valid. The existing repository test pattern covers every other write method; omitting these two creates a gap in the regression net precisely where isolation bugs would be highest impact.

title: embeddings_handler missing ensure_model_inference guard — inference grant bypass
issue: The `embeddings_handler` function does not call `auth_scope.access_policy().ensure_model_inference(&model)` before forwarding the request to the upstream provider. Every other inference surface in this diff received the guard — `chat_completions_handler` (line 134), `anthropic_messages_create_handler` (line 148), and `gemini_action_handler` (line 213) — but the embeddings endpoint was missed. The policy check is the only layer that enforces per-model inference grants for `ApiToken` and grant-bound `ExternalApp` principals.
failure_scenario: Token created with `grants: TokenGrantsV1 { models: ModelGrant::Specific { ids: ["allowed-model"] }, mcps: McpGrant::Specific { ids: [] } }` sends `POST /v1/embeddings {"model": "restricted-model", "input": "hello"}`. The handler resolves the alias via `find_alias("restricted-model")` and forwards to the upstream provider without any grant check. A 200 embedding response is returned. The same token calling `POST /v1/chat/completions` with the same model correctly receives 403.
recommendation: Add `auth_scope.access_policy().ensure_model_inference(&model)?;` immediately after the model is extracted from the request (line ~255), before `find_alias` is called. Mirror the comment from `chat_completions_handler`: 'Check before resolution: a non-granted model must 403, not leak existence via 404.' Also add a unit test parallel to `test_chat_completions_scoped_token_forbidden` and `test_chat_completions_external_app_model_forbidden` to cover both ApiToken and ExternalApp restricted cases.
rationale: All three inference surfaces touched in this diff were guarded by `ensure_model_inference` except the embeddings handler. A direct comparison of the diff for `routes_oai_chat.rs` shows the guard was only added to `chat_completions_handler`; `embeddings_handler` in the same file was not updated. The grant system's contract — 'Specific ids = deny all others' — is violated for this endpoint.

title: Anthropic model list/get handlers bypass model-listable grant filter
issue: Neither `anthropic_models_list_handler` nor `anthropic_models_get_handler` applies `auth_scope.access_policy().model_listable()` filtering before returning model metadata. The OAI-compatible `/v1/models` list and get handlers were correctly updated in this diff (`oai_models_handler` line 89-90, `oai_model_handler` line 141). The Anthropic model discovery endpoints were missed, creating a discrepancy: a restricted token returns an empty list from `GET /v1/models` but receives the full Anthropic model catalog (including any custom-prefixed alias names) from `GET /anthropic/v1/models`.
failure_scenario: Token created with `models: Specific { ids: [] }` (zero model inference rights) calls `GET /anthropic/v1/models`. The handler iterates all Anthropic-format aliases and returns the full list of model entries with their aliased IDs (e.g. `org-prefix/claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022`). The same token calling `GET /v1/models` receives an empty list. The token cannot use the models for inference (the messages create handler correctly enforces `ensure_model_inference`), but it learns the complete Anthropic model configuration from the discovery endpoint.
recommendation: After building the `ordered` list in `anthropic_models_list_handler`, apply `let policy = auth_scope.access_policy(); ordered.retain(|m| m.get("id").and_then(|v| v.as_str()).is_some_and(|id| policy.model_listable(id)));`. In `anthropic_models_get_handler`, add `if !auth_scope.access_policy().model_listable(&model_id) { return Err(AnthropicApiError::not_found(&model_id)); }` before the alias scan, mirroring `oai_model_handler`'s 404-on-not-listable pattern to avoid revealing existence to scoped tokens.
rationale: The diff shows that `anthropic_messages_create_handler` had `ensure_model_inference` added but the two Anthropic model discovery handlers were not updated. The OAI equivalent handlers were both fixed in the same diff. The grant system documents that 'listing is separate from inference: with listing off, discovery returns only individually granted resources' — this contract is not honoured for the Anthropic surface.

title: Gemini model list/get handlers bypass model-listable grant filter
issue: Neither `gemini_models_list` nor `gemini_models_get` applies `model_listable` grant filtering. `gemini_action_handler` (line 213) was correctly updated in this diff to call `ensure_model_inference`. The list and get handlers were not updated, creating the same OAI-vs-provider inconsistency as the Anthropic finding: `GET /v1/models` respects grants, `GET /v1beta/models` and `GET /v1beta/models/{id}` do not.
failure_scenario: Token with `models: Specific { ids: [] }` calls `GET /v1beta/models`. The handler iterates all Gemini-format aliases and returns all model entries with their prefixed `name` fields (e.g. `models/org-prefix/gemini-1.5-pro`). The same token calling `GET /v1/models` returns empty. The token cannot call `:generateContent` or `:streamGenerateContent` on the models (the action handler enforces `ensure_model_inference`), but the discovery endpoint reveals the full Gemini model configuration.
recommendation: After building `ordered` in `gemini_models_list`, add `let policy = auth_scope.access_policy(); ordered.retain(|m| m.get("name").and_then(|v| v.as_str()).and_then(|n| n.strip_prefix("models/")).is_some_and(|id| policy.model_listable(id)));` — or extract the key used in `seen` (the `prefix + model_id` form) before inserting to `ordered`. In `gemini_models_get`, add the analogous not-listable 404 guard before the alias scan.
rationale: The diff for `routes_gemini.rs` shows only `gemini_action_handler` was updated with `ensure_model_inference`; `gemini_models_list` and `gemini_models_get` in the same file were left without grant filtering. This is the same missed-surface pattern as the Anthropic handlers.

title: No grant-enforcement tests for embeddings, Anthropic model discovery, or Gemini model discovery
location: entire file; also test_anthropic_models.rs and test_gemini_routes.rs
issue: The test files for the three surfaces with missing grant enforcement (embeddings, Anthropic model list/get, Gemini model list/get) have zero tests exercising `ApiToken` or `ExternalApp` grant restriction. `test_embeddings.rs` only uses `AuthContext::test_session(ResourceRole::User)` — Unrestricted — meaning the missing `ensure_model_inference` guard passed CI undetected. `test_anthropic_models.rs` and `test_gemini_routes.rs` similarly contain no restricted-grant test cases. Compare with `test_chat_completions.rs` which has `test_chat_completions_scoped_token_forbidden` and `test_chat_completions_external_app_model_forbidden` that would have caught this class of bug immediately.
failure_scenario: N/A — test-coverage gap. The guard omission in `embeddings_handler` (finding 1) was not caught by CI because no test asserts that a scoped `ApiToken` or grant-bound `ExternalApp` receives 403 from `POST /v1/embeddings` with a non-granted model.
recommendation: Add to `test_embeddings.rs`: (a) `test_embeddings_scoped_token_forbidden` — `ApiToken` with `ModelGrant::Specific { ids: ["some-other-model"] }` calling `POST /v1/embeddings` with `model = "testalias-exists:instruct"` expects 403. (b) `test_embeddings_external_app_model_forbidden` — same with `ExternalApp` + `ApprovedResourcesV1 { models_access: Specific { ids: [...] } }`. Add analogous tests to `test_anthropic_models.rs` (`test_anthropic_models_list_scoped_token_filtered`) and `test_gemini_routes.rs` (`test_gemini_models_list_scoped_token_filtered`) asserting that a token with zero model grants receives an empty list, not the full catalog.
rationale: The model-grant enforcement tests in `test_chat_completions.rs` serve as the pattern; those tests were added in this diff. The same pattern was not applied to the other inference and discovery surfaces, leaving three handlers untested for grant enforcement.

title: McpGrant::None removed without DB migration — management UI shows incorrect all-access fallback for legacy tokens
issue: The `McpGrant::None` variant (serialized as `{"type":"none"}`) was introduced in the first commit of this diff (`4dea5ea9`) and removed in the Phase-0 commit (`f1265209`). No database migration was added to convert existing `{"type":"none"}` rows. The two deserialization sites have opposite failure modes: the middleware (token_service.rs line 137) fails CLOSED — `serde_json::from_str` failure causes a 401 rejection, preventing token use. The management read path (line 192) uses `unwrap_or_default()`, so the same corrupt row is silently displayed as all-access (`ModelGrant::All`, `McpGrant::All`) in the token management UI. Any token that was created with `McpGrant::None` during the window between Phase 2 deployment and Phase 0 deployment cannot be used (correctly rejected) but appears fully unrestricted in the UI (incorrect).
failure_scenario: A token was created with explicit `mcps: McpGrant::None` via the Phase 2 token creation API, storing `{"version":"1","list_models":false,"models":{"type":"all"},"list_mcps":false,"mcps":{"type":"none"}}`. After Phase 0 is deployed: (a) the token is presented to the middleware → `serde_json::from_str` fails → 401 `Invalid grants: unknown variant none` — token stops working. (b) the admin lists tokens via `GET /bodhi/v1/tokens` → `From<TokenEntity>` calls `unwrap_or_default()` → the token is shown with `mcps: {type: all}` in the API response, misrepresenting its configured grants.
recommendation: Add a custom deserializer for `McpGrant` (or a migration) that maps `{"type":"none"}` to `Specific { ids: [] }`. Alternatively, add a SeaORM migration that runs a SQL UPDATE on the `api_tokens` table converting any `grants` column values containing `"type":"none"` to `"type":"specific","ids":[]`. The middleware deserialization is correctly fail-closed and does not need to change; only the display path and stored data need the compat shim.
rationale: The diff removes `McpGrant::None` from grant_objs.rs/token_objs.rs (Phase 0 commit) and introduces the `From<TokenEntity>` with `unwrap_or_default()` fallback in the same diff. There is no accompanying migration file in the diff. The asymmetry between the middleware's fail-closed behavior and the management API's fail-open display means administrators can be shown misleading grant data for tokens created during the transition window.

title: page.evaluate() for clipboard read is a black-box violation; line-64 assertion is vacuous
issue: `copyTokenFromDialog()` clicks the copy button then reads the token value back with `this.page.evaluate(() => window.clipboardData)`, violating the black-box rule ('no page.evaluate for test assertions', feedback_blackbox_e2e). More critically, the spec at api-tokens.spec.mjs:64 asserts `expect(clipboardContent).toBe(copiedToken)` where both `clipboardContent` (clipboard.getContent()) and `copiedToken` (copyTokenFromDialog()) are obtained by reading the SAME `window.clipboardData` JS global via evaluate — the assertion is trivially true and proves nothing. If the copy button failed silently, both would return `''` and the assertion would still pass.
failure_scenario: The copy button silently stops invoking navigator.clipboard.writeText (e.g. due to a missing onclick handler). Both `copiedToken` and `clipboardContent` return `''`, `expect(clipboardContent).toBe(copiedToken)` passes vacuously. The only meaningful assertion (`expect(copiedToken).toBe(tokenValue)` at line 65) compares `''` to the real token value read earlier from the DOM — that assertion WILL catch the bug, but only because line 65 crosses sources (DOM vs. evaluate). The test structure is fragile: removing line 65 (or reading `tokenValue` via evaluate too) would leave zero enforcement.
recommendation: Remove `page.evaluate()` from `copyTokenFromDialog()`. Instead return the value from `clipboard.getContent()` that the caller already owns, or read the token value from the UI (the shown `data-testid='token-value-input-content'` div). Replace the `waitForTimeout(100)` with `await expect(clipboard.getContent()).not.toBe('')` so Playwright polls until the async write completes. Remove the now-redundant line 64 assertion from the spec.
rationale: feedback_blackbox_e2e.md bars page.evaluate for assertions. Two reads from the same JS global yield a tautological test that can mask copy failures.

title: Two waitForTimeout() calls in TokensPage violate the no-inline-timeout rule
issue: `copyTokenFromDialog()` has `await this.page.waitForTimeout(100)` before the clipboard evaluate, and `toggleTokenStatus()` has `await this.page.waitForTimeout(100)` after `waitForSPAReady()`. Both violate E2E.md ('No page.waitForTimeout() or setTimeout()'). The first masks a race between the click and the async clipboard write; the second masks a flaky UI state after a toggle.
failure_scenario: On a slow CI agent the clipboard write takes >100 ms; `evaluate()` reads stale `''`; `copyTokenFromDialog()` returns `''`; the spec assertion `expect(copiedToken).toBe(tokenValue)` fails with a confusing mismatch rather than a deterministic Playwright actionability failure. For `toggleTokenStatus()`, the 100 ms sleep may be insufficient on a loaded agent, causing subsequent `findTokenByName()` to see the pre-toggle DOM and report the wrong status.
recommendation: In `copyTokenFromDialog()`: replace the `waitForTimeout` with a polling `expect` on the clipboard or with the approach above (read from UI). In `toggleTokenStatus()`: replace the `waitForTimeout` with `await expect(statusSwitch).toHaveAttribute('aria-checked', ...)` polling the switch aria state, or use `expectTokenStatus()` which already uses `expect.poll()`.
rationale: E2E.md: 'No page.waitForTimeout() or setTimeout(). Wait for UI state changes via data-testid/data-test-state attributes with Playwright's built-in waiting.'

title: reducedMotion:'reduce' not set before the 'Scoped grants' step that opens the detail rail
location: Token Lifecycle test, 'Scoped grants' step starting at line 106; openTokenRail() call at line 130
issue: `page.emulateMedia({ reducedMotion: 'reduce' })` is called at line 137 inside the 'List behaviors' step, but the preceding 'Scoped grants' step calls `tokensPage.openTokenRail()` at line 130 which triggers the V2 rail screen with a view-transition animation. The feedback_e2e_reduced_motion_for_view_transitions rule requires this setting to be applied BEFORE the first test step that opens the rail, to prevent view-transition overlay detach races.
failure_scenario: On a machine with a display refresh cycle that allows the view-transition to run, `openTokenRail()` triggers the cross-fade; while the overlay is attached, `expectModelGrantChip()` at line 131 fires before the rail content is fully detached from the transition, intermittently returning 'element not visible' even though the chip is present in the DOM.
recommendation: Move `await page.emulateMedia({ reducedMotion: 'reduce' })` to before the 'Scoped grants' step (e.g. immediately after setup login), or add it as the first line inside 'Scoped grants'. The plan (mellow-porcupine Phase 4) also calls for this setting to be configured at the page-object / fixture level rather than inside individual steps.
rationale: feedback_e2e_reduced_motion_for_view_transitions.md: 'V2 rail screens: set reducedMotion:reduce to kill view-transition detach races; wait for mutation settle before asserting.'

title: selectSpecificFromPanel() missing Sheet overlay detach wait, causing potential click interception on next action
issue: After clicking the panel Done button, `selectSpecificFromPanel()` returns immediately without waiting for the Sheet overlay to detach. `AccessRequestReviewPage.setModelAccessSpecific()` (line 60) correctly waits: `await this.page.locator('[data-testid="review-model-access-panel"]').waitFor({ state: 'hidden' })`. In `createTokenWithGrants()` where both `specificModels` and `specificMcps` are true, the model-access Sheet may still be animating when `accessModeSpecific('mcp-access')` is clicked, causing the overlay to intercept the click. Similarly, the mcp-access Sheet may still be open when `selectScope()` and `generateButton.click()` fire.
failure_scenario: In the 'Scoped grants' E2E step, `createTokenWithGrants({ specificModels: true, specificMcps: true, ... })` is called. The model-access panel's closing animation runs for ~200 ms; `selectSpecificFromPanel('mcp-access')` clicks `[data-testid='mcp-access-mode-specific']` while the model-access Sheet backdrop is still in the DOM and intercepting pointer events. Playwright reports 'Element is intercepted by another element' or the click lands on the backdrop instead, causing the MCP panel never to open and `items.first().waitFor()` to time out.
recommendation: After `accessPanelDone.click()`, add `await this.page.locator(this.selectors.accessPanel(prefix)).waitFor({ state: 'hidden' })` before returning, mirroring the pattern in `AccessRequestReviewPage.setModelAccessSpecific()`.
rationale: The same component (shadcn Sheet) is used in both review and token pages; the same overlay-detach race exists in both paths. The review page already has the correct fix; parity is required here.

title: Phase 9 missing: positive model-inference enforcement (granted model infers successfully)
location: Entire spec — no positive inference step
issue: The spec only approves an empty-Specific grant (no models) to get a deterministic 403. Plan Phase 9 (prom-lexical-wren) explicitly requires 'granted model infers, other model 403'. The positive enforcement path — where the owner grants exactly one model and the exchanged token successfully calls `/v1/chat/completions` with that model — is never tested. AccessPolicy's `allows_model_inference()` for `ApprovedResourcesV1 { models: Specific { ids: ["m1"] } }` is not covered by any E2E test.
failure_scenario: A bug in `ResourceGrants for ApprovedResourcesV1::allows_model_inference()` causes it to return `false` even for an explicitly granted model id (e.g. off-by-one in `ids.contains()` after an ids canonicalization change). The current spec only checks that an empty grant yields 403 — which would still pass. The granted-model-infers path goes undetected in E2E.
recommendation: Add a second owner-consent path that grants a specific registered model (register one via `registerApiModelViaUI` as setup). After token exchange, call `/v1/chat/completions` with the GRANTED model and assert 200 (or a real completion with a real key). Call `/v1/chat/completions` with a DIFFERENT model and assert 403. This mirrors the API-token 'Chat Integration' step in api-tokens.spec.mjs.
rationale: Plan Phase 9 (prom-lexical-wren): 'granted model infers, other model 403' is an explicit deliverable. The current test covers only one arm of a two-arm requirement.

title: Phase 9 missing: MCP grant enforcement and /v1/mcps list-toggle tests
location: Entire spec — no MCP enforcement steps
issue: Despite requesting `mcps_list: true` and `mcps_access: true` in the access-request payload, the spec never: (a) tests that `/v1/mcps` responds correctly when `list_mcps=true` vs `list_mcps=false`; (b) attempts an MCP connect on a granted instance to confirm 200; (c) attempts an MCP connect on an ungranted instance to confirm 403. Phase 9 (prom-lexical-wren) explicitly requires 'granted MCP connects, other 403' and '/v1/mcps reflect toggles'.
failure_scenario: A regression in `ResourceGrants for ApprovedResourcesV1::allows_mcp_connect()` (e.g. the `mcps_extra` union path is broken) causes all MCP connections to fail even for granted instances. The current spec never makes an MCP connection call, so this regression is invisible in E2E.
recommendation: Register an Everything MCP instance as setup. In the owner-approval step, grant it via `mcps_access: true` and select the instance id. After token exchange, call the MCP proxy endpoint for the granted instance and assert 200; call it for an ungranted instance and assert 403. Also call `GET /v1/mcps` and assert that the list includes only the granted instance (reflecting `list_mcps` toggle).
rationale: Plan Phase 9 (prom-lexical-wren): 'granted MCP connects, other 403; /v1/models & /v1/mcps reflect toggles' are explicit deliverables. Zero MCP enforcement is tested end-to-end.

title: Keyboard-nav focus target inlines internal CSS class .l-listrow.active in the spec
issue: The spec directly constructs the focus target as `tokensPage.selectors.tokensTable + ' .l-listrow.active [data-testid="row-link"]'`, coupling the spec to the internal CSS class `.l-listrow.active`. The plan (mellow-porcupine Phase 4) required a `selectRowByKeyboard(dir)` page-object method to encapsulate this. Accessing `selectors.tokensTable` from the spec bypasses the page-object abstraction layer; the `.active` CSS selector is also fragile (a class rename in the shared catalog CSS would silently break the focus target, causing `focus()` to silently no-op on a non-matching empty locator).
failure_scenario: N/A — convention issue. If `.l-listrow` is renamed to `.l-row` in the shared catalog table CSS, `locator(...).focus()` matches nothing (Playwright waits until timeout) rather than giving a descriptive failure, obscuring the root cause.
recommendation: Add a `TokensPage.focusActiveRow()` method (or `pressKeyOnList(key)`) that encapsulates `this.page.locator(this.selectors.tokensTable + ' .l-listrow.active [data-testid="row-link"]').focus()`, exposing it through the page object. The spec then calls `await tokensPage.focusActiveRow()` — the selector change is a single-point fix.
rationale: Plan Phase 4 (mellow-porcupine): 'add ... selectRowByKeyboard(dir)' as a page-object method. Specs should not reach into implementation selectors.
